title: Lesson 279
transcript: Music playing We've talked about risk management and the three process that we go through in risk management, identifying our most valuable assets, identifying the risks associated with those assets, and then treating those risks. And we've even talked in a little more detail about how we treat risks. We can avoid, we can transfer, We can mitigate and we can accept. Let's now get into a bit more detail on how we actually mitigate the risk, where we try and reduce the risk by implementing controls. And so let's talk through those controls that we can implement, because there's a number of different ones. And let's start with directive controls. So what is directive sounds like? Well, it's directing someone's behavior, getting them to do a certain thing in a certain way. How do we direct employees' behavior within an organization? Well, through our corporate laws, through policies. So essentially, directive controls are policies which direct behavior. Next category of controls are what are known as deterrent controls. So what's a deterrent? Let's look at a couple of examples that'll give you a sense of what deterrent controls are. So an example from physical security, a deterrent control would be not the fence, but the sign that says, You know, there's hungry dogs beyond this fence or you'll be prosecuted to the full extent of the law if you enter this restricted area. That deters someone from entering the property, but does it prevent them? it doesn't so that's a deterrent control it deters another example from logical security would be a warning banner so when you log into a computer system and there's that warning banner that comes up that says this is a confidential system and you'll be prosecuted to the full extent of the law if you misuse this system something like a warning banner like that is another example of a deterrent control. Now, what about preventative controls? Preventative controls actually prevent someone from doing something. So if we think about logical security again, an example of a preventative control would be, say, a fence, or landmines, or hungry dogs. Although, don't use those last two, because the number one goal of physical security is safety of people. landmines especially, not good for safety of people. And what about if we continue the analogy within logical security? What would be an example of a preventative control within logical security? Well, the actual login mechanism where you have to provide the correct username and password, otherwise you are prevented from logging into the system. Now, as much as possible, we would like to direct away from, you know, deter, prevent risks from occurring, but we can never prevent everything from occurring. That's why we need detective controls. Detective controls will detect if a risk has occurred. So this would be examples from physical security would be something like a camera system. In logical security, it'd be things like IDS, IPS type systems. These are detective controls that detect if a risk has occurred. As soon as we've detected that a risk has occurred, we want to try and minimize, mitigate the damage of that risk, and that's where corrective controls come in. Corrective controls try and minimize the damage. So an example of a corrective control in say physical security would be your fire suppression system. If you've detected that there's a fire, your corrective control would be say your water-based fire suppression system, which is gonna flood the water, flood water down into the room to put out to correct the fire. And this actually is a good thing to think about because corrective controls, while they try and minimize the damage, they certainly don't get us back to business as usual. If our fire suppression system is just activated, our room is gonna be flooded with filthy water out of these years old pipes. And so we're certainly not back to business as usual. And that's where recovery controls come in. Recovery controls are controls that we put in place to get us back to business as usual, back to normal operations. So clean up the mess, get everything reinstalled, running again, recovery controls. The final control is what's known as compensating controls. Compensating controls are controls that we put in place where we don't have a better control or a better control would be far too expensive. And so compensating controls are things like supervisory reviews, maybe encrypting some data or doing things like segregation of duties. So that's compensating controls. Now, there's one final important concept that we should cover here, and I want you to watch the animation here very carefully because it took me way too long to figure out how to animate this in PowerPoint. There's three of these controls that are critically important and we refer to them as being a complete control. So we always, whenever we're implementing controls, we always want to have a complete control and a complete control is a combination of preventive detective and corrective because we'd like to try and prevent everything that we possibly can for those risks that we can't prevent we want to be able to detect them as quickly and efficiently as possible and then correct those risks. So we refer to that as a complete control. And later on when we're talking about defense in depth and layering and any of these concepts, the idea of a complete control is that at every single layer you want to implement a complete control, a combination of preventive, detective, and corrective. Thank you.
